text.title: stakeholder-analysis Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for stakeholder-analysis. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: List every party who is affected by, has influence over, must approve, will implement, or will use the solution.
rule.rationale: Missing a stakeholder early guarantees conflicting or incomplete requirements arriving late.

rule.statement: Document influence, impact, attitude, knowledge, needs, and communication style for each identified stakeholder.
rule.rationale: Without per-stakeholder attributes, engagement is generic and misses resistance early.

rule.statement: Place stakeholders on a 2x2 power-interest grid: Manage Closely (high/high), Keep Satisfied (high/low), Keep Informed (low/high), Monitor (low/low).
rule.rationale: Grid quadrant determines engagement cadence and channel; without this, effort is spread evenly across unequal stakeholders.

rule.statement: For each key stakeholder, record specific needs, concerns, success criteria, and current pain points.
rule.rationale: Stated needs alone are insufficient; unstated concerns drive resistance if not surfaced early.

rule.statement: Assign an engagement approach (Active/Consult/Inform), frequency, and method to each stakeholder based on their grid quadrant.
rule.rationale: A single plan for all stakeholders wastes sponsor time and under-invests in high-impact resistors.

rule.statement: Treat stakeholder analysis as a living activity: re-run the salience classifier after org events (reorg, leadership change, regulatory letter).
rule.rationale: BAKOK 3 explicitly calls stakeholder analysis a continuous activity; static artifacts go stale in 2-6 weeks.

rule.statement: Use Mendelow for engagement cadence (which quadrant maps to which channel), not for prioritization.
rule.rationale: Mendelow flattens politics to two axes and is silent on legitimacy and urgency; it is a channel selector, not a prioritization model.

rule.statement: Apply Mitchell-Agle-Wood (power x legitimacy x urgency) to classify each stakeholder into one of 8 types: dormant, discretionary, demanding, dominant, dangerous, dependent, definitive, non-stakeholder.
rule.rationale: Salience tells you who matters and in what order; Mendelow tells you which channel to use. They are complementary, not substitutes.

rule.statement: Require cited evidence (quote, transcript line, org-chart entry) for every salience axis; return "unknown" if evidence is absent — never infer urgency from tone alone.
rule.rationale: LLM urgency inference from tone is a known failure mode; a regulatory deadline or contractual SLA is the only valid urgency signal.

rule.statement: Pick ONE responsibility model per program before any artifact is produced; document the choice and its rationale.
rule.rationale: RACI, RASCI, DACI, and RAPID use some of the same letters with different meanings; mixing models within a program makes every role assignment ambiguous.

rule.statement: Enforce RACI integrity mechanically: exactly one Accountable per row, at least one Responsible, no R+A collapse to a single person. Lint in CI.
rule.rationale: Human-edited RACI tables drift toward multiple A entries and R-spam; a linter is cheaper than an audit finding.

rule.statement: Keep stakeholder, persona, and actor artifacts strictly separate: a stakeholder row never becomes a persona; an actor (UML) is a system-interaction role with no political weight; a persona has no decision authority.
rule.rationale: Conflating them breaks requirements traceability; a persona that inherits engagement cadence from a stakeholder produces meaningless engagement plans.

rule.statement: Maintain an explicit non-stakeholders list. Documenting exclusions prevents repeated re-litigation of who belongs on the register.
rule.rationale: Without a declared exclusion list, every new team member re-opens the same boundary debates.

rule.statement: Before classifying any stakeholder, persist a glossary.md and model-choice.md in the program BA folder declaring: stakeholder definition (verbatim from BABOK 3 §3.2 or ISO 21502 §4.4), responsibility model chosen, salience model chosen, and rationale for rejecting alternatives.
rule.rationale: LLMs treat "stakeholder" as a synonym for "user" or "persona" by default; injecting the frozen definition verbatim into the system prompt is the only reliable mitigation.

rule.statement: Sponsor signs the register baseline; subsequent diffs require sponsor acknowledgment only for attitude or accountability changes, not for contact info updates.
rule.rationale: Keeps sign-off lightweight while protecting the high-stakes fields that affect engagement strategy.
